items.title: China’s Dark Factories: So Automated, They Don't Need Lights | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Ford’s CEO says China’s EV industry poses an existential threat to global automakers. The country’s dark factories, which have robots run 24/7 with limited human interaction,  aim to further widen the gap between Chinese car manufacturers and everyone else. As China grapples with overproduction and Trump’s trade war with Beijing continues, one big question remains: who will buy all these vehicles?

WSJ visited Zeekr, a Chinese luxury EV-maker, to learn about China’s hyper-automation.

Chapters:
0:00 China’s dark factories
0:40 China’s labor costs
1:19 Inside Zeekr
2:21 China’s automation
3:13 U.S. playing catchup 
4:13 Challenges in the Chinese economy

News Explainers
Some days the high-speed news cycle can bring more questions than answers. WSJ’s news explainers break down the day's biggest stories into bite-size pieces to help you make sense of the news.

#China #EV #WSJ</article>

items.title: Medicaid, Explained: What’s Trump Cutting in 2025? | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Medicaid began as a welfare program, but 60 years later it covers one in five Americans and costs more than $800 billion. But Trump’s “Big Beautiful Bill” is changing the health insurance program. 

WSJ explores how Medicaid got so big and how the GOP megabill will change it.

Chapters:
0:00 How big is Medicaid?
0:32 Private health insurance
1:16 Why Medicaid was started
3:02 Breaking down the gaps
4:00 Trump’s megabill changes

Some days the high-speed news cycle can bring more questions than answers. WSJ’s news explainers break down the day's biggest stories into bite-size pieces to help you make sense of the news.

#Medicaid #Trump #WSJ</article>

items.title: Why Russia Is Doubling Down in Ukraine While Trump Calls for Peace | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Despite President Trump’s calls for peace with Putin, Russia is ramping up its summer offensive on Ukraine. Trump announced a deal with NATO to send weapons and ammunition to Ukraine, including the Patriot missile defense system, as Moscow has ramped up its drone attacks amid Kyiv’s depleting defenses. 

WSJ explains what’s at stake and why the next months will be critical in the conflict. 

Chapters:
0:00 Russia’s offensive
0:33 Air attacks and Trump’s reaction
2:16 The Sumy region 
3:41 Ukraine’s defensive capabilities 
5:13 What’s next?

Russia-Ukraine Conflict
WSJ’s latest news coverage around the 2022-2025 Russia-Ukraine conflict.

#Russia #Ukraine #WSJ</article>

items.title: What ESG Funds and Anti-Woke Funds Have in Common
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>What do anti-woke funds and ESG funds have in common? Conservative exchange-traded funds like the American Conservative Values ETF promise to align portfolios with political beliefs, avoiding companies seen as hostile to their values. But some of these funds fall into the same pitfalls that tripped up socially conscious investments.

#ETFs #Investing #Shorts</article>

items.title: Why Trump’s Next Fed Chair Transition Could Be Completely Different | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>President Donald Trump has signaled he’s already looking to replace Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell far earlier than usual. Here’s how a so-called ‘shadow’ Fed would work, and what it means for markets and consumers.

items.title: Hunting Russian Spies in Norway’s ‘Spy Town’ | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Kirkenes, a Norwegian town on the border with Russia, has become a target for espionage operations. The town’s proximity to Russia and nearby NATO bases has made it a target for Russian spies taking photos of military infrastructure, doing covert maritime intelligence operations and more. 

WSJ follows an agent from Norway’s domestic intelligence agency, the PST, on the hunt for illegal activity.

Chapters:
0:00 Russia’s shadow war
0:55 Russian threats 
2:25 Ports and Russian vessels
5:05 Interviewing a retired border guard
7:07 Current border guards
7:51 Recruitment and Wagner
10:05 Inside the town

items.title: Why the U.S. Quitting Artificial Food Dyes Is So Difficult | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>RFK Jr. says it is working with the food industry to remove synthetic food dyes from the food supply by the end of 2026. But dye makers say meeting this deadline safely could take more than six years.

WSJ visited Sensient Technologies, one of the largest natural food color plants in the world, to understand why the 2026 deadline is so hard to achieve and what it takes to create natural food dyes. 

Chapters:
0:00 Natural food dye transitions
0:25 Industry change
1:34 Challenges 
4:25 State legislation

#RFK #Food #WSJ</article>

items.title: Why So Much Russian Military Infrastructure Sits in This Narrow Bay | WSJ Coordinates
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>The Kola Bay is emerging as one of Russia’s most powerful hubs in its quest to dominate the Arctic. This small stretch of water is where Moscow stores part of its icebreaker fleet and could mine for minerals and oil, export those resources and launch nuclear weapons. 

WSJ explores why Kola Bay is so critical for Russia’s fight for the Arctic. 

Chapters:
0:00 Russia’s Arctic ambitions
0:54 Why the Kola Bay is so critical
2:30 Russia’s nuclear weapons and icebreaker ships
5:36 How other countries are responding

Coordinates
In Coordinates, WSJ explains the geopolitics and developments of key regions imperative to the military strategies of countries around the world.

#Russia #Arctic #WSJ</article>

items.title: Ibogaine: Can an African Root Help Solve the U.S. Opioid Crisis?
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>The opioid crisis continues to claim lives and devastate communities. A little-known powerful psychedelic called ibogaine has been touted for its remarkable ability to erase years of addiction and withdrawal in just one session. But ibogaine remains illegal in the U.S., causing thousands of Americans to cross borders into places like Mexico and Brazil to receive treatment. WSJ visits a clinic called Ambio Life Sciences in Tijuana, Mexico, to follow one patient's journey through this intense and unorthodox treatment.

#Opioid #Drug #Shorts</article>

items.title: How Water Makes This Town Flood-Proof | WSJ Pro Perfected
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Many Florida homes can withstand category 5 hurricane winds, but not flooding. Babcock Ranch, a town near Fort Myers and Cape Coral, has stayed mostly unscathed during major storms like Hurricane Irma, Ian, Milton and Helene. 

WSJ spoke with the town’s engineer to uncover the hurricane-proofing designs that help protect it.

Chapters:
0:00 Hurricane-proof town?
0:39 Storm-water management
2:52 Elevation
4:20 Location of the town
5:53 Florida’s vulnerability

Pro Perfected
Experts in engineering and design break down a ubiquitous problem, examining how the world is built and what can make it better.

#Flood #Florida #WSJ</article>

items.title: How Labubu Skyrocketed Pop Mart Stock 1,200%
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Chinese toy company Pop Mart’s stock exploded over 1,200% in the past year thanks to its success it built off blind box collectibles like Labubu. These hard-to-get toys drive the majority of Pop Mart’s revenue as unboxings have flooded social media and have fans flocking overseas to buy exclusive products. But how is the retailer making itself more than just a trend? WSJ explores how Pop Mart is harnessing the craze to create IP longevity and looks at how the company has ambitious growth plans beyond this viral moment. 

#PopMart #Labubu #Shorts</article>

items.title: The Pyro Drones Transforming Fireworks Displays
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>millisecond accuracy. These high-tech displays require extensive engineering, choreographed flight plans with 3D animation software and sophisticated GPS systems. But these massive shows aren’t without risks as crashing drones can be a real concern. WSJ explores the technology behind how the quadcopter fleets are changing aerial displays.

#Fireworks #July4th #Shorts</article>

items.title: Trump’s Big Beautiful Bill: The Tax and Spending Updates, Explained | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>The House of Representatives passed President Trump’s one big, beautiful tax and spending bill. The sweeping domestic policy megabill includes Medicaid cuts, Trump’s promised no tax on tips and clean energy tax credit changes. The bill also increases government spending on ICE and border security, missile defense, shipbuilding and more.

WSJ’s Richard Rubin breaks down the final version of the tax-and-spending megabill passed by Congress before Trump signs it.

Chapters:
0:00 The House passes Trump’s megabill
0:20 Tax-cut extensions 
1:31 New tax deductions
2:44 Spending increase on defense and border security 
3:22 Medicaid, SNAP and clean energy
4:50 How the bill increases the deficit

Some days the high-speed news cycle can bring more questions than answers. WSJ’s news explainers break down the day's biggest stories into bite-size pieces to help you make sense of the news.

#Trump #Taxes #WSJ</article>

items.title: Why the U.S. Military’s Big Problem Is Chinese-Made Cargo Ships
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>China is a global shipbuilding powerhouse — and it builds most of America’s cargo ships. President Trump and Congress want to change that with the passing of Trump’s big, beautiful bill. If the U.S. went to war with Beijing over Taiwan, it could struggle to resupply troops due to a lack of U.S.-built cargo ships. WSJ’s Stu Woo breaks down two steps the U.S. is taking to reverse this trend.

#China #Trump #Shorts</article>
